### Title

Correlation Between Average Token Entropy and Character Error Rate

### Purpose of the Figure

This figure provides an alternative view of the correlation between average token entropy and character error rate, complementing [figure_04.md](figure_04.md). It uses Pearson and Spearman correlation coefficients. In each quartile, a small number of high-entropy, high-CER samples strongly influence Pearson but not Spearman. Spearman better reflects the trend across the full distribution.

### Generated By

[stratified_analysis.py](../../scripts/stratified_analysis.py)

### Input Files Used

[results_k_10.csv](../../results/csv/results_k_10.csv)

### Output Filenames

PNG: [figure_05_stratified_correlations.png](../../results/figures/figure_05_stratified_correlations.png)

SVG: [figure_05_stratified_correlations.svg](../../results/figures/figure_05_stratified_correlations.svg)

### X-axis

Ground-Truth Length (Characters) Quartile

### Y-axis

Correlation

### Units

Correlation (blue bar): Pearson

Correlation (orange bar): Spearman

### Other Information

Abbreviations: “n” refers to the number of samples in each quartile, and “QX” refers to the quartile index.

Filtering rules: samples with a CER > 2% were filtered from the dataset.   

Normalization choices: whitespace was normalized, punctuation was removed entirely, and all characters were converted to lowercase for both ground-truth and OCR texts.

Sample size: the plot consists of 100 randomized page excerpts stratified into four quartiles.

### Figure Importance

This figure shows that Pearson correlation is sensitive to a small number of high-entropy, high-CER samples within each quartile, and that Spearman is a more appropriate metric for this analysis.
